title: Today's Tech Brief: Bye-bye, Gemini CLI; Google's gone and swapped you for a closed-source AI
summary: Google discontinues Gemini CLI, shifting to a closed-source AI, while classrooms increasingly adopt analog learning methods.
imageAlt: Image from source article for Bye-bye, Gemini CLI; Google's gone and swapped you for a closed-source AI
imageCaption: Bye-bye, Gemini CLI; Google's gone and swapped you for a closed-source AI
imageSourceTitle: Bye-bye, Gemini CLI; Google's gone and swapped you for a closed-source AI

body.text: Google is sunsetting its Gemini CLI, a popular tool for developers, in favor of a closed-source AI solution called 'AntiGravity'. Most users will lose access on June 18, unless they have enterprise credentials or paid API keys. This move has raised eyebrows, as Gemini CLI was widely used and appreciated for its open-source nature.

The reason behind this shift is unclear, but it's likely due to Google's desire to control the AI ecosystem and generate revenue through its paid offerings. AntiGravity seems to be Google's attempt to create a more proprietary, user-friendly experience. However, this change may leave some developers scrambling for alternatives.

In a separate development, classrooms are increasingly adopting analog learning methods in the AI era. According to Axios, educators are leaning into traditional teaching tools, possibly as a response to the growing presence of AI in education. I find it interesting that, in an age of AI-driven innovation, there's a renewed focus on low-tech solutions.

As I look at these developments, I'm struck by the contrast between Google's move towards closed-source AI and the resurgence of analog learning. It seems that, while some are betting on AI to revolutionize industries, others are finding value in simplicity. My read is that we're in a period of experimentation, where companies and educators are trying to find the right balance between technology and traditional methods.
